summary: O'Neil examines a New York Times article about bipartisan justice reform efforts, connecting it to evidence-based sentencing practices she's researched extensively. She notes that data-driven approaches to reducing recidivism can either perpetuate inequality by penalizing poverty or genuinely improve resource allocation. The unusual coalition of Koch Industries and the ACLU working together on this issue gives her rare optimism, though she cautions that reformers must understand that models aren't inherently objective. She argues data systems could actively fight unfairness rather than codify existing biases.
key_points: Evidence-based sentencing approaches range from harmful (punishing poverty) to helpful (focusing resources effectively)
The Koch-ACLU coalition on justice reform represents a rare moment of bipartisan potential
Data-driven models in justice are not inherently objective and require people who understand modeling to implement them properly
Data systems can be designed to actively combat unfairness rather than simply automate existing discriminatory practices
money_quotes: This stuff runs the gamut between putting poor people away for longer because they're poor and actually focusing resources where they're needed.
I'm desperately hoping they have involved people who understand enough about modeling not to assume that the results of models are 'objective'.

There are, in fact, lots of ways to set up data-gathering and usage in the justice system to actively fight against unfairness and unreasonably long incarcerations, rather than to simply codify such practices.
key_insight: Data-driven justice reform can either entrench inequality or fight it, depending entirely on whether the people implementing it understand that models are not inherently objective.
